blocks.html:
EXPOSÉ V
\(\ell\)-ADIC PROJECTIVE SYSTEMSby
J. P. JOUANOLOU
Throughout the exposé, the letter \(A\) denotes a ring with unit.
The present exposé is intended to develop a formalism which will be used in the following exposé for the study of \(\ell\)-adic cohomology.
1. Generalities on abelian \(A\)-categories.

blocks.title: DEFINITION
blocks.html:
DEFINITION 1.1. — We call a \(A\)-category (resp. abelian \(A\)-category) a pair \((C,\rho)\) formed of an additive (resp. abelian) category \(C\) and a ring homomorphism:
\[ \rho:A\longrightarrow\operatorname{End}(\operatorname{id}_C) \]

from \(A\) into the ring of endomorphisms of the identity functor of \(C\) into \(C\).

Given two \(A\)-categories (resp. abelian \(A\)-categories) \((C,\rho)\) and \((C',\rho')\), we call a morphism of \(A\)-categories (resp. of abelian \(A\)-categories from \((C,\rho)\) into \((C',\rho')\)) an additive (resp. exact) functor \(F:C\longrightarrow C'\) such that for every object \(M\) of \(C\) and every element \(a\) of \(A\) the equality below is satisfied:

\[ F(\rho(a)_M)=\rho'(a)_{F(M)}. \]

Example 1.1.1. Let \(C\) be an additive (resp. abelian) category. Recall that we call the category of (left) \((A,C)\)-modules, and denote by \((A,C)\text{-}\mathrm{mod}\), the category described as follows:
(i) Its objects are the pairs \((X,\alpha)\) formed of an object \(X\) of \(C\) and a ring morphism \(\alpha:A\longrightarrow\operatorname{Hom}_C(X,X)\).
(ii) A morphism \((X,\alpha)\longrightarrow(Y,\beta)\) is a \(C\)-morphism \(u:X\longrightarrow Y\) such that for every element \(a\) of \(A\) the diagram below is commutative:
\[ \xymatrix{ X\ar[r]^u\ar[d]_{\alpha(a)}&Y\ar[d]^{\beta(a)}\\ X\ar[r]^u&Y. } \]

The category \((A,C)\text{-}\mathrm{mod}\) is obviously a \(A^\natural\)-category (resp. abelian \(A^\natural\)-category), where \(A^\natural\) is the center of \(A\). Moreover, it satisfies the following properties: \(a\) The functor \((A,C)\text{-}\mathrm{mod}\longrightarrow C\) obtained by forgetting the \(A\)-module structure commutes with inductive and projective limits.

\(b\) Let \(I\) be a category. For the \(I\)-inductive limits (resp. projective limits) of \((A,C)\text{-}\mathrm{mod}\) to be representable, it suffices that this be so for the \(I\)-inductive (resp. projective) limits of \(C\).
1.2. External tensor product.

Let \((C,\rho)\) be an abelian \(A\)-category. If \(M\) is a right \(A\)-module and \(X\) an object of \(C\), we call the external tensor product of \(X\) by \(M\), and denote by \(M\otimes_A X\), the covariant functor from \(C\) into the category of sets, defined on the objects \(Y\) of \(C\) by the equality below and in the obvious way on the arrows:
\[ (M\otimes_A X)(Y)=\operatorname{Hom}_A(M,\operatorname{Hom}_C(X,Y)), \]

where \(\operatorname{Hom}_C(X,Y)\) is regarded as a right \(A\)-module thanks to the action of \(A\) on \(X\). Interpreting \(M\otimes_A X\) and \(Y\) as objects of the category \(\widehat C=\operatorname{Hom}(C,\mathrm{Ens})^\circ\), the preceding equality takes the more familiar form:
\[ \operatorname{Hom}_{\widehat C}(M\otimes_A X,Y) =\operatorname{Hom}_A(M,\operatorname{Hom}_C(X,Y)). \]
If the functor \(M\otimes_A X\) is representable, we shall denote and name in the same way a representative.

If \(u:M\longrightarrow M'\) and \(v:X\longrightarrow X'\) are two morphisms of \((A\text{-}\mathrm{mod})\) and \(C\) respectively, one defines in the usual way a \(C\)-morphism \(u\otimes_A v:M\otimes_A X\longrightarrow M'\otimes_A X'\), which will allow us to speak of the (twice covariant) external tensor product bifunctor. Its properties are summarized in the following proposition.

blocks.title: PROPOSITION
blocks.html: PROPOSITION 1.2.1. — The tensor product bifunctor is additive and commutes with inductive limits.
The proof, classical in the case of modules over a ring, is immediate.

blocks.title: PROPOSITION
blocks.html:
PROPOSITION 1.2.2. (Representability criteria).

\(a\) For every object \(X\) of \(C\), \(A\otimes_A X\) is represented by \(X\).

\(b\) For every \(A\)-module \(M\) of finite presentation and every object \(X\) of \(C\), the functor \(M\otimes_A X\) is representable.

\(c\) If the category \(C\) possesses "arbitrary" direct sums, the functor \(M\otimes_A X\) is representable for "every" pair \((M,X)\).
Proof: Assertion \(a\) is clear. The two other assertions then follow from the lemma below, whose proof is immediate.

blocks.title: LEMMA
blocks.html: LEMMA 1.2.3. — Let \(M\longrightarrow N\longrightarrow P\longrightarrow0\) be an exact sequence of \(A\)-modules and \(X\) an object of \(C\). If the functors \(M\otimes_A X\) and \(N\otimes_A X\) are represented by objects \(Y\) and \(Z\) of \(C\), the functor \(P\otimes_A X\) is represented by the cokernel of the morphism \(Y\longrightarrow Z\) corresponding to \(\alpha\otimes_A\operatorname{id}_X\).
Remark 1.2.4. Let \(\mathfrak a\) be an ideal of finite type of \(A\). For every object \(X\) of \(C\), the functor \((A/\mathfrak a)\otimes_A X\) is representable, and the arrow
\[ p_X:X\longrightarrow(A/\mathfrak a)\otimes_A X \]

coming from the canonical epimorphism of \(A\) onto \(A/\mathfrak a\) and from part \(a\) of 1.2.2 is an epimorphism. The same statement is valid when the ideal \(\mathfrak a\) is no longer assumed of finite type, but \(C\) possesses "arbitrary" direct sums. We set \(\mathfrak aX=\ker(p_X)\). When the category possesses filtered inductive limits, it is clear that \(\mathfrak aX=\sum_{a\in\mathfrak a}aX\). If \(u:X\longrightarrow Y\) is an epimorphism, \(u\) sends \(\mathfrak aX\) epimorphically onto \(\mathfrak aY\). Finally, for an object \(X\) to be killed by every element of \(\mathfrak a\), it is necessary and sufficient that \(\mathfrak aX=0\). Then every morphism from an object \(Z\) into \(X\) factors by means of \(p_Z\) through a morphism from \((A/\mathfrak a)\otimes_A Z\) into \(X\).
2. Mittag-Leffler-Artin-Rees condition.

Throughout this paragraph, the letter \(C\) denotes an abelian category and we set \(P=\operatorname{Hom}(\mathbb N^\circ,C)\), the category of projective systems indexed by the ordered set \(\mathbb N\) of positive integers, with values in \(C\).
2.1. Mittag-Leffler-Artin-Rees condition.

Let \(X=(X_n,u_n)_{n\geq0}\) be an object of the category \(P\). If \(r\) denotes an integer \(\geq0\), we agree to denote by \(X[r]\) the projective system \((X_{n+r},u_{n+r})_{n\geq0}\). A morphism \(h:X\longrightarrow Y\) of \(P\) defines in an obvious way a morphism denoted \(h[r]\) from \(X[r]\) into \(Y[r]\), and this in a functorial way. If now \(r\) and \(s\) are two integers satisfying the inequalities \(s\geq r\geq0\), the "transition morphisms" \(X_{n+s}\longrightarrow X_{n+r}\) \((n\geq0)\) define a morphism \(w_{sr}\) from \(X[s]\) into \(X[r]\), and it is clear that if \(t\) is a third integer with \(t\geq s\geq r\), the equality below is satisfied:
\[ w_{ts}\circ w_{sr}=w_{tr}. \]

In other words, we have thus associated to \(X\) an object \(\overline X\) of the category \(\operatorname{Hom}(\mathbb N^\circ,P)\). Moreover, if \(h:X\longrightarrow Y\) is a morphism of \(P\), there corresponds to it in an obvious way a morphism \(\overline h\) from \(\overline X\) into \(\overline Y\), and this in a functorial way.

Finally, if \(0\longrightarrow X\xrightarrow{u}Y\xrightarrow{v}Z\longrightarrow0\) is an exact sequence of \(P\), the corresponding sequence below in \(\overline P=\operatorname{Hom}(\mathbb N^\circ,P)\) is exact:
\[ 0\longrightarrow\overline X\xrightarrow{\overline u}\overline Y \xrightarrow{\overline v}\overline Z\longrightarrow0. \]

blocks.title: DEFINITION
blocks.html:
DEFINITION 2.1.1. — Let there be an object of the category \(P\). We say that it satisfies the Mittag-Leffler-Artin-Rees condition (abbreviated MLAR) if there exists an integer \(r\) such that for every integer \(s\geq r\) the equality below is satisfied:
\[ \operatorname{Im}(X[s]\xrightarrow{w_{s0}}X) =\operatorname{Im}(X[r]\xrightarrow{w_{r0}}X). \]

It amounts to the same thing to say that for every integer \(n\geq0\) the images of \(X_{n+r}\) and \(X_{n+s}\) in \(X_n\) under the "transition morphisms" are the same. Finally, a third interpretation consists in saying that the associated projective system \(\overline X\) satisfies the Mittag-Leffler condition (abbreviated ML) (EGA \(0_{\mathrm{III}}\) 13.1.2). From this we deduce the following proposition thanks to (EGA \(0_{\mathrm{III}}\) 13.2.1).

blocks.title: PROPOSITION
blocks.html:
PROPOSITION 2.1.2. — Let \(0\longrightarrow X\longrightarrow Y\longrightarrow Z\longrightarrow0\) be an exact sequence of \(P\).

(i) If \(Y\) satisfies (MLAR), \(Z\) satisfies (MLAR).

(ii) If \(X\) and \(Z\) satisfy (MLAR), then \(Y\) satisfies (MLAR).
2.2. AR-null projective systems.

blocks.title: DEFINITION
blocks.html: DEFINITION 2.2.1. — Let \(X\) be an object of the category \(P\). We say that \(X\) is AR-null if there exists an integer \(r\geq0\) such that the canonical morphism \(X[r]\longrightarrow X\) is null.
It is clear in particular that an AR-null projective system satisfies the condition (MLAR).

blocks.title: PROPOSITION
blocks.html:
PROPOSITION 2.2.2. — Let \(0\longrightarrow X\longrightarrow Y\longrightarrow Z\longrightarrow0\) be an exact sequence of \(P\). The following assertions are equivalent:

(i) \(Y\) is AR-null,

(ii) \(X\) and \(Z\) are AR-null.
In other words, the full subcategory \(P_0\) of \(P\), whose objects are the AR-null projective systems, is thick in \(P\).

Proof: It is clear that (i) implies (ii). Conversely, let \(r\) and \(t\) be integers satisfying the condition of definition (2.2.1) for \(X\) and \(Z\) respectively. One sees at once that the integer (s+t) meets the conditions of definition (2.2.1) for \(Y\).
2.3. Reminders on the calculus of fractions and applications.

Let \(E\) be a category and \(S\) a subset of \(\operatorname{Fl}(E)\). We call a category of fractions of \(E\) with respect to \(S\) a pair \((p,E(S^{-1}))\) formed of a category \(E(S^{-1})\) and a functor \(p:E\longrightarrow E(S^{-1})\) satisfying the following universal property:

For a functor \(u\) from \(E\) into a category \(F\) to factor through \(p\), it is necessary and sufficient that it render invertible the arrows belonging to \(S\), and then the factorization is unique.

The uniqueness of such a pair up to isomorphism is clear. We refer the reader to exposé I of the Séminaire Homotopique de Strasbourg for the proof of existence, as well as, moreover, for most of the results stated without proof in the rest of the paragraph. (See also: P. Gabriel and M. Zisman, Calculus of fractions and homotopy theory, Erg. der Math., 35, Springer-Verlag, 1967.)
Certain properties of the multiplicative subset \(S\) ensure a simple description of the category \(E(S^{-1})\):

blocks.title: DEFINITION
blocks.html:
DEFINITION 2.3.1. — We say that a subset \(S\) of \(\operatorname{Fl}(E)\) permits a calculus of right fractions if the following conditions are realized:

\(a\) The identity arrows of \(E\) belong to \(S\).

\(b\) If \(u:X\longrightarrow Y\) and \(v:Y\longrightarrow Z\) belong to \(S\), the composite \(v\circ u\) belongs to \(S\).

\(c\) Every diagram of type \((D_1)\) below, in which \(t\) belongs to \(S\), can be completed into a diagram of type \((D_2)\), in which \(s\) belongs to \(S\).

\[ \begin{array}{c@{\qquad\qquad}c} (D_1)\quad\xymatrix{X\ar[r]^u&Y&Y'\ar[l]_t} & (D_2)\quad\xymatrix{X'\ar[r]^{u'}\ar[d]_s&Y'\ar[d]^t\\X\ar[r]_u&Y} \end{array} \]

\(d\) For every commutative diagram:

\[ \xymatrix{X\ar@<.5ex>[r]^f\ar@<-.5ex>[r]_g&Y\ar[r]^s&Z,} \]

in which \(s\) belongs to \(S\), there exists a commutative diagram

\[ \xymatrix{T\ar[r]^t&X\ar@<.5ex>[r]^f\ar@<-.5ex>[r]_g&Y} \]

in which \(t\) belongs to \(S\).
Dually one defines the following notion:

blocks.title: DEFINITION
blocks.html:
DEFINITION 2.3.2. — We say that a subset \(S\) of \(\operatorname{Fl}(E)\) permits a calculus of left fractions if the following conditions are realized:

\(a\)' The identity arrows of \(E\) belong to \(S\).

\(b\)' The composite of two arrows belonging to \(S\) belongs to \(S\).

\(c\)' Every diagram of type \((D_1)'\) below, in which \(s\) belongs

to \(S\), can be completed into a diagram of type \((D_2)'\), in which \(t\) belongs to \(S\):

\[ \begin{array}{c@{\qquad\qquad}c} (D_1)'\quad\xymatrix{X'\ar[r]^{u'}\ar[d]_s&Y'\\X} & (D_2)'\quad\xymatrix{X'\ar[r]^{u'}\ar[d]_s&Y'\ar[d]^t\\X\ar[r]_u&Y} \end{array} \]

\(d\)' For every commutative diagram (Delta) of the form below, in which \(s\) belongs to \(S\), there exists a morphism \(t\) with source \(Y\) and belonging to \(S\) such that \(t\circ f=t\circ g\).

\[ (\Delta)\quad\xymatrix{X'\ar[r]^s&X\ar@<.5ex>[r]^f\ar@<-.5ex>[r]_g&Y} \]

2.3.3. Suppose that \(S\) is a subset of \(\operatorname{Fl}(E)\) permitting a calculus of right fractions. It then follows from part \(d\) of (2.3.1) that for every object \(X\) of \(E\), the category \((S/X)^\circ\), opposite to the category of arrows over \(X\) belonging to \(S\), is filtered (SGA 4 I 2.7). Let us then consider the category \(E_S\) described as follows:
– The objects of \(E_S\) are the objects of \(E\),
– If \(X\) and \(Y\) are two objects of \(E\),
\[ \operatorname{Hom}_{E_S}(X,Y)= \underset{(S/X)^\circ}{\operatorname{Lim}} \operatorname{Hom}_E(\operatorname{source}(\mathord\cdot),Y). \]

If \(p_S\) denotes the obvious functor from \(E\) into \(E_S\), the pair \((p_S,E_S)\) is a solution of the universal problem described at the beginning of the paragraph. We shall agree in this case to denote by \(E(S^{-1})\) this particular solution.

2.3.4. Let now \(S\) be a subset of \(\operatorname{Fl}(E)\) permitting a calculus of left fractions. Then the functor \(p_S:E\longrightarrow E(S^{-1})\) commutes with finite inductive limits. In particular, if \(E\) is additive, \(E(S^{-1})\) is additive and \(p_S\) is an additive functor.

2.3.5. If the category \(E\) is abelian and if \(S\) is a subset of \(\operatorname{Fl}(E)\) permitting both a calculus of right fractions and a calculus of left fractions, the category \(E(S^{-1})\) is abelian and the functor \(p_S\) is exact. Moreover the pair \((p_S,E(S^{-1}))\) is a quotient of \(E\) by a thick abelian subcategory \(p_S^{-1}(0)\).
2.4. The category \(P_{AR}=\operatorname{Hom}_{AR}(\mathbb N^\circ,C)\).

Resuming the notations of (2.1), let (AR) be the set of canonical arrows (cf. 2.1) \(v_{rX}:X[r]\longrightarrow X\), \(X\) running through the set of objects of \(P\) and \(r\) the set of integers (geq0).

blocks.title: PROPOSITION
blocks.html: PROPOSITION 2.4.1. — The subset (AR) of \(\operatorname{Fl}(P)\) permits both a calculus of right fractions and a calculus of left fractions.
Proof: Parts \(a\) ⇒ \(a\)' and \(b\) ⇒ \(b\)' of (2.3.1) and (2.3.2) are clear.
Let us show \(c\). Let then \((D_1)\) be the commutative diagram below, in which \(v_{rY}\) denotes the canonical arrow from (Y[r]) into \(Y\):
\[ (D_1)\quad\xymatrix{&Y[r]\ar[d]^{v_{rY}}\\X\ar[r]&Y} \]
The diagram \((D_2)\) below is commutative and \(v_{rX}\), the canonical morphism from (X[r]) into \(X\), belongs to (AR), whence the assertion.
\[ (D_2)\quad\xymatrix{X[r]\ar[r]^{u[r]}\ar[d]_{v_{rX}}&Y[r]\ar[d]^{v_{rY}}\\X\ar[r]_u&Y} \]
Let us show \(d\). Let there be a commutative diagram:
\[ \xymatrix{X\ar@<.5ex>[r]^f\ar@<-.5ex>[r]_g&Z[r]\ar[r]^{v_{rZ}}&Z.} \]
Applying the "translation" by \(r\), we deduce from it a commutative diagram:
\[ \xymatrix{X[r]\ar@<.5ex>[r]^{f[r]}\ar@<-.5ex>[r]_{g[r]}&Z[2r]\ar[r]^{v_{rZ}[r]}&Z[r].} \]
It is clear that the diagram below is commutative, so that \(f\circ v_{rX}=g\circ v_{rX}\):
\[ \xymatrix{X[r]\ar@<.5ex>[r]^{f[r]}\ar@<-.5ex>[r]_{g[r]}\ar[d]_{v_{rX}}&Z[2r]\ar[d]^{v_{rZ}[r]}\\X\ar@<.5ex>[r]^f\ar@<-.5ex>[r]_g&Z[r].} \]

Let us show \(c'\). Let us remark first of all that if \(Y\) denotes an object of \(P\) and \(r\) a positive integer, there exists an object \(T\) of \(P\) such that \(Y=T[r]\) and such that for every object \(X\) of \(P\), the homomorphism of translation by \(r\):
\[ \operatorname{Hom}_P(X,T)\longrightarrow\operatorname{Hom}_P(X[r],Y) \]
is a bijection. The projective system \((T_n,w_n')\) defined as follows obviously works:

\[ \begin{aligned} &\text{If }Y=(Y_n,u_n)_{n\geq0},\quad T_n\text{ is the zero object of }C \text{ for }n\geq r-1\text{ and }Y_{n-r}\text{ otherwise},\\ &w_n'=0\text{ for }n\geq r-1,\quad w_n'=u_{n-r}. \end{aligned} \]
This said, suppose given a diagram of the type below, and let us show that it can be completed as indicated in the statement of \(c'\):
\[ \xymatrix{X[r]\ar[r]^{u'}\ar[d]_{v_{rX}}&Y'\\X.} \]

For this, let \(Y\) be a projective system such that \(Y'=Y[r]\) and that the canonical map \(\operatorname{Hom}_P(X,Y)\longrightarrow\operatorname{Hom}_P(X[r],Y')\) is a bijection. Then it is clear that the diagram below is commutative, \(u\) denoting the unique morphism from \(X\) into \(Y\) such that \(u'=u[r]\):
\[ \xymatrix{X[r]\ar[r]^{u'}\ar[d]_{v_{rX}}&Y'=Y[r]\ar[d]^{v_{rY}}\\ X\ar[r]^u&Y.} \]
Let us show \(d\)'. Consider the diagram below, assumed commutative, and let us show that there exists a morphism \(w\) of \(Y\) such that \(w\circ f=w\circ g\).
\[ \xymatrix{X[r]\ar[r]^{v_{rX}}&X\ar@<.5ex>[r]^f\ar@<-.5ex>[r]_g&Y.} \]
It is first of all clear that the diagram below is commutative:
\[ \xymatrix{X[r]\ar@<.5ex>[r]^{f[r]}\ar@<-.5ex>[r]_{g[r]}\ar[d]_{v_{rX}}& Y[r]\ar[d]^{v_{rY}}\\X\ar@<.5ex>[r]^f\ar@<-.5ex>[r]_g&Y.} \]
In particular \(v_{rY}\circ f[r]=v_{rY}\circ g[r]\). Let now \(T\) be the system associated to \(Y\) and \(r\) as at the beginning of the proof of \(c\)', so that in

particular \(Y=T[r]\). The translates by \(r\) of the morphisms \(v_{rT}\circ f\) and \(v_{rT}\circ g\) are equal respectively to \(v_{rY}\circ f\) and \(v_{rY}\circ g[r]\), hence equal. It then follows from the fact that the translation by \(r\) is an injection on the morphisms from \(X\) into \(T\) that the equality below is satisfied, whence the assertion:
\[ v_{rT}\circ f=v_{rT}\circ g. \]

DEFINITION 2.4.2. Let \(C\) be an abelian category. We call the category of projective systems of \(C\) up to translations, and denote by \(\operatorname{Hom}_{AR}(\mathbf N^\circ,C)\), the category described below:
(i) The objects of \(\operatorname{Hom}_{AR}(\mathbf N^\circ,C)\) are the objects of \(\operatorname{Hom}(\mathbf N^\circ,C)\).

(ii) If \(X\) and \(Y\) are two objects of \(\operatorname{Hom}(\mathbf N^\circ,C)\), a morphism from \(X\) into \(Y\) in the category \(\operatorname{Hom}_{AR}(\mathbf N^\circ,C)\) is an element of the set
\[ \operatorname{Hom}_{AR}(X,Y)=\underset{\longrightarrow r}{\lim} \operatorname{Hom}(X[r],Y), \]
where, for every pair \((r,s)\) of integers with \(s\geq r\), the map
\[ \operatorname{Hom}(X[r],Y)\longrightarrow\operatorname{Hom}(X[s],Y) \]
comes from the canonical morphism
\[ X[s]\longrightarrow X[r]. \]
The composition of morphisms is defined in the obvious way.
In what follows, the category \(C\) being fixed, we shall set for simplicity \(P=\operatorname{Hom}(\mathbf N^\circ,C)\) and \(P_{AR}=\operatorname{Hom}_{AR}(\mathbf N^\circ,C)\).
2.4.3. We have seen (2.4.1) that the subset \(AR\) of \(Fl(P)\) permits a calculus of right fractions. This allows us (2.3.3) to consider the category:
which, we recall, has the same objects as \(P\).

Let \(X\) and \(Y\) be two objects of \(P\), and let us denote by \(\operatorname{Hom}'_{AR}(X,Y)\) the set of isomorphisms from \(X\) into \(Y\) in \(P'_{AR}\). An element of \(\operatorname{Hom}_{AR}(X,Y)\) defines in an obvious way an element of \(\operatorname{Hom}'_{AR}(X,Y)\). More precisely, one defines in this way a functor
\[ m:P_{AR}\longrightarrow P'_{AR}. \]
PROPOSITION 2.4.3. The functor \(m\) is an isomorphism of categories.
Proof: It is a matter of seeing that for every pair \((X,Y)\) of objects of \(P\), the corresponding map
\[ m_{XY}:\operatorname{Hom}_{AR}(X,Y)\longrightarrow \operatorname{Hom}'_{AR}(X,Y) \]

is a bijection. It is obviously a surjection. To see the injectivity, one reduces immediately to seeing that for every commutative diagram of the type below, there exists an integer \(t\) greater than or equal to \(r\) and \(s\) such that the composite of \(\rho\) and the canonical morphism from \(X[t]\) into \(X[s]\) is the canonical morphism from \(X[t]\) into \(X[r]\).
\[ \xymatrix{X[r]&X[s]\ar[l]_\rho\\&X\ar[ul]^{v_{rX}}\ar[u]_{v_{sX}}} \]
Now this follows from the commutativity of the diagram below, in which we have designated by the letter \(c\) all the canonical morphisms coming into play.
\[ \xymatrix{X[r+s]&X[2s]\ar[l]_{\rho(s)}\ar[d]^c\\ X[r]\ar[u]^c&X[s]\ar[l]^\rho\ar[u]_c} \]
PROPOSITION 2.4.4.
(i) The category \(P_{AR}\) is abelian.
(ii) The functor \(P_{AR}:P\longrightarrow P_{AR}\) is exact.
(iii) For an object \(X\) of \(P\) to be AR-null, it is necessary and sufficient that \(P_{AR}(X)=0\).
(iv) The pair \((P_{AR},P_{AR})\) is a quotient of the abelian category \(P\) by the thick abelian subcategory of AR-null objects.

Proof: If one admits (iii), the other assertions are a consequence of (2.3.5). It follows from (2.3.4) that for an object \(X\) of \(P\) to be AR-null, it is necessary and sufficient that \(P_{AR}(\operatorname{id}_X)=0\), whence (iii).
COROLLARY 2.4.5. Let \(u:X\longrightarrow Y\) be of the category \(P\). The following assertions are equivalent:
(i) \(P_{AR}(u)\) is an isomorphism.
(ii) The kernel and the cokernel of \(u\) are AR-null.
A morphism \(u:X\longrightarrow Y\) of the category \(P\) of projective systems, satisfying the equivalent conditions (i) and (ii), will be called an AR-isomorphism.
DEFINITION 2.4.6. Two objects \(X\) and \(Y\) of the category \(P\) are said to be AR-isomorphic if \(P_{AR}(X)\) and \(P_{AR}(Y)\) are isomorphic in the category \(P_{AR}\).
If \(X\) and \(Y\) are two AR-isomorphic objects of the category \(P\), there does not in general exist an AR-isomorphism \(u\) from \(X\) into \(Y\).
2.5. Characterization of projective systems satisfying the Mittag-Leffler-Artin-Rees condition.
PROPOSITION 2.5.1. The following assertions for an object \(X\) of \(P\) are equivalent:
(i) \(X\) satisfies (MLAR) (2.1.1).
(ii) There exist a strict projective system \(Y\) and an AR-isomorphism \(u:Y\longrightarrow X\) (2.4.5).
(iii) There exists a strict projective system \(Y\) such that \(X\) and \(Y\) are isomorphic in the category \(P_{AR}\).
(iv) \(X\) satisfies the Mittag-Leffler condition and, denoting by \(X'\) the projective system of universal images of \(X\), the projective system \(X/X'\) is AR-null.
Proof: We are going to prove the chain of implications:
\[ (i)\Rightarrow(iv)\Rightarrow(iii)\Rightarrow(ii)\Rightarrow(i). \]

\((i)\Rightarrow(iv)\). There exists an integer \(m\) such that the canonical morphism \(w_{mX}\) from \(X[m]\) to \(X\) factors through the canonical inclusion \(i\) of \(X'\) into \(X\). It follows that \(P_{AR}(i)\) is a (direct) epimorphism, hence, since \(P_{AR}\) is exact, that \(P_{AR}(X/X')=0\). We conclude by (2.4.4 (iii)).

\((iv)\Rightarrow(iii)\). The condition of (iv) means, with the preceding notations, that \(P_{AR}(i)\) is an epimorphism. But because of the exactness of \(P_{AR}\), it is even a monomorphism, hence an isomorphism. For the object \(Y\) we take the projective system \(X'\).

\((iii)\Rightarrow(ii)\). The \(P_{AR}\)-isomorphism from \(Y\) to \(X\) whose existence is assumed is the image under \(P_{AR}\) of an \(P\)-morphism \(Y[r]\longrightarrow X\), where \(r\) denotes a nonnegative integer. We conclude thanks to the fact that \(Y[r]\) is strict.

\((ii)\Rightarrow(i)\). We may assume \(Y\) is contained in \(X\) and that \(u\) is the canonical injection. Let \(T\) be the cokernel of \(u\). The hypothesis made means that \(T\) is AR-null, so there exists an integer \(r\) such that for every \(s\) greater than or equal to \(r\) the canonical morphism from \(T[s]\) to \(T\) is zero. We conclude that for \(s\geq r\) the image of \(X[s]\) in \(X\) is contained in \(Y\); as on the other hand it obviously contains the image, equal to \(Y\), of \(Y[s]\) in \(Y\), it is equal to \(Y\). We conclude that \(Y\) is the image of \(X[s]\)
for \(s\) sufficiently large, hence that \(X\) satisfies (MLAR) and that \(Y\) is the projective system of universal images of \(X\).

COROLLARY 2.5.2. Let \(u:Y\longrightarrow X\) be an AR-isomorphism, with \(Y\) strict. Under these conditions, \(X\) satisfies condition (MLAR) and \(u(Y)\) is the projective system of universal images of \(X\).
3. \(J\)-adic and AR-\(J\)-adic projective systems.

Let \(A\) be a commutative ring with unit, \(J\) an ideal of \(A\), \((C,\rho)\) an abelian \(A\)-category. We assume either that \(J\) is of finite type, or that \(C\) possesses infinite direct sums. The notations are the same as in the preceding paragraph. In particular, the symbols \(P\) and \(P_{AR}\) denote respectively the categories \(\operatorname{Hom}(\mathbb N^\circ,C)\) and \(\operatorname{Hom}_{AR}(\mathbb N^\circ,C)\).
3.1. \(J\)-adic projective systems.
DEFINITION 3.1.1. Let \(X\) be an object of \(P\). We say that \(X\) is \(J\)-adic if it satisfies the following conditions:
(i) For every integer \(n\geq0\), \(J^{n+1}X_n=0\).

(ii) For every pair \((m,n)\) of integers with \(m\geq n\geq0\), the morphism \[ A/J^{n+1}\otimes_A X_m\longrightarrow X_n \] deduced from the transition morphism \(X_m\longrightarrow X_n\) is an isomorphism.
We denote by \(J\text{-}\mathrm{ad}(C)\) the full subcategory of \(P\) generated by the \(J\)-adic projective systems.

Remark 3.1.2. Let \(I\) and \(J\) be two ideals of \(A\) and assume either that \(I\) and \(J\) are of finite type, or that \(C\) admits infinite direct sums. Then one can show that if \(I\) and \(J\) define the same topology on \(A\), the categories \(I\text{-}\mathrm{ad}(C)\) and \(J\text{-}\mathrm{ad}(C)\) are equivalent.
PROPOSITION 3.1.3. (Stability under exact sequences). Let
\[ 0\longrightarrow X\xrightarrow{u}Y\xrightarrow{v}Z\longrightarrow0 \]
be an exact sequence of \(P\).
(i) If \(X\) is strict and \(Y\) is \(J\)-adic, then \(Z\) is \(J\)-adic.
(ii) If \(Y\) is strict and \(Z\) is \(J\)-adic, then \(X\) is strict.
(iii) If \(X\) and \(Z\) are \(J\)-adic, \(Y\) is \(J\)-adic.
Proof: If \(m\geq n\), it is clear that the diagram \(T\) below, in which the vertical arrows denote the transition morphisms, is commutative and exact:

\[ \tag{T} \xymatrix{ A/J^{n+1}\otimes_A X_m\ar[r]^{\mathrm{id}\otimes u_m}\ar[d]_{f_{nm}}& A/J^{n+1}\otimes_A Y_m\ar[r]^{\mathrm{id}\otimes v_m}\ar[d]_{g_{nm}}& A/J^{n+1}\otimes_A Z_m\ar[r]\ar[d]_{h_{nm}}&0\\ 0\ar[r]&X_n\ar[r]&Y_n\ar[r]&Z_n\ar[r]&0. } \]
In case (i), \(f_{nm}\) is an epimorphism and \(g_{nm}\) an isomorphism.
In case (ii), \(g_{nm}\) is an epimorphism and \(h_{nm}\) an isomorphism.
In case (iii), \(f_{nm}\) and \(h_{nm}\) are isomorphisms. In all three cases, the snake lemma applied to the diagram \(T\) allows us to conclude.
3.2. AR-\(J\)-adic projective systems.
PROPOSITION 3.2.1. The following assertions, for an object \(X\) of \(P\) such that \(J^{n+1}X_n=0\) for every \(n\geq0\), are equivalent:
(i) There exist a \(J\)-adic projective system \(Y\) and an AR-isomorphism \(u:Y\longrightarrow X\) (2.4.5).
(ii) There exists a \(J\)-adic projective system \(Y\), isomorphic to \(X\) in the category \(P_{AR}\).

Proof: We must show that (ii) implies (i). An \(P_{AR}\)-isomorphism from \(Y\) to \(X\) is represented for some integer \(r\) by an AR-isomorphism \(v:Y[r]\longrightarrow X\). Since \(J^{n+1}X_n=0\) for every \(n\geq0\) and \(Y\) is \(J\)-adic, \(v\) is the composite of an \(P\)-morphism \(u:Y\longrightarrow X\) and the canonical morphism from \(Y[r]\) to \(Y\). It is then clear that \(u\) is an AR-isomorphism, whence the assertion.
DEFINITION 3.2.2. A projective system indexed by \(\mathbb N\) with values in \(C\) is called AR-\(J\)-adic if:
\(a\) \(J^{n+1}X_n=0\) for every \(n\geq0\),
\(b\) it satisfies the equivalent conditions (i) and (ii) of proposition (3.2.1).

PROPOSITION 3.2.3. Let \(X\) be a projective system indexed by \(\mathbb N\) with values in \(C\). We assume that \(J^{n+1}X_n=0\) for every \(n\geq0\). Then for \(X\) to be AR-\(J\)-adic, it is necessary and sufficient that it satisfy property (MLAR) and that, denoting by \(X'\) its projective system of universal images, there exist an integer \(r\geq0\) such that, for every pair \((m,n)\) of integers with \(m\geq n+r\), the "transition morphism" below is an isomorphism:
\[ X'_m/J^{n+1}X'_m\longrightarrow X'_{n+r}/J^{n+1}X'_{n+r}. \]
Proof:
\(a\) The conditions of the statement are sufficient:

If \(X\) satisfies (MLAR), it is AR-isomorphic to its projective system of universal images \(X'\). On the other hand, the hypothesis made on \(X'\) means that the projective system \((X'_{n+r}/J^{n+1}X'_{n+r})_{n\geq0}\) (the transition morphisms being obvious) is \(J\)-adic. Now it is clear that this projective system is AR-isomorphic (2.4.6) to \(X'\).
\(b\) The conditions of the statement are necessary: if \(X\) is AR-\(J\)-adic, it is AR-isomorphic to a strict projective system, hence satisfies (MLAR).

The projective system \(X'\) of universal images of \(X\) is AR-isomorphic to \(X\), hence AR-\(J\)-adic. To prove the second point, we are thus reduced to seeing that if \(X\) is an AR-\(J\)-adic and strict projective system, there exists an integer \(r\) such that the projective system \(\ell_r(X)\) defined by \[ \ell_r(X)_n=X_{n+r}/J^{n+1}X_{n+r} \] is \(J\)-adic. For this, let \(u:Y\longrightarrow X\) be an AR-isomorphism, with \(Y\) \(J\)-adic. The kernel \(T\) of \(u\) being AR-null, let \(r\) be an integer such that the canonical morphism \(T[r]\longrightarrow T\) is zero. It is immediate that the canonical morphism \(\ell_r(u):\ell_r(Y)\longrightarrow\ell_r(X)\) is an isomorphism, whence the assertion.
PROPOSITION 3.2.4. (Stability under exact sequences). Let
\[ 0\longrightarrow X\xrightarrow{u}Y\xrightarrow{v}Z\longrightarrow0 \]
be an exact sequence of \(P\), the projective systems \(X,Y\) and \(Z\) being such that
(i) If \(X\) satisfies (MLAR) and \(Y\) is AR-\(J\)-adic, \(Z\) is AR-\(J\)-adic.
(ii) If \(Y\) satisfies (MLAR) and \(Z\) is AR-\(J\)-adic, \(X\) satisfies (MLAR).
Proof: We freely use, for every projective system \(X\) and for every integer \(r\), the notation \(\ell_r(X)\) already made explicit.

Proof of (i): If \(X'\) and \(Y'\) denote the projective systems of universal images of \(X\) and \(Y\), \(u\) sends \(X'\) into \(Y'\), whence the commutative exact diagram below, in which the arrows are obvious:
\[ \xymatrix{ 0\ar[r]&X'\ar[r]\ar[d]&Y'\ar[r]\ar[d]&\widetilde Z'\ar[r]\ar[d]&0\\ 0\ar[r]&X\ar[r]&Y\ar[r]&Z\ar[r]&0. } \]
Since the category \(P_{AR}\) is abelian and the functor
\[ P_{AR}:P\longrightarrow P_{AR} \]

is exact, it follows from this diagram, and from the fact that the inclusions of \(X'\) and \(Y'\) into \(X\) and \(Y\) respectively are AR-isomorphisms (2.5.1), that the morphism \(\widetilde Z\longrightarrow Z\) is an AR-isomorphism.

This allows us to reduce to the case where \(X\) and \(Y\), hence \(Z\), are strict. There then exists an integer \(r\) such that the projective system \(\ell_r(Y)\) is \(J\)-adic. The functor \(\ell_r\) is obviously right exact, whence an exact sequence
\[ \ell_r(X)\xrightarrow{\ell_r(u)}\ell_r(Y) \xrightarrow{\ell_r(v)}\ell_r(Z)\longrightarrow0. \]
Replacing \(\ell_r(X)\) if necessary by its image under \(\ell_r(u)\), we are in the conditions of application of (3.1.3 (i)), which shows that \(\ell_r(Z)\) is \(J\)-adic, whence the assertion.

(ii) Let \(Y'\) and \(Z'\) be the projective systems of universal images of \(Y\) and \(Z\). Consider the obvious commutative exact diagram below, in which the two right-hand vertical arrows are the canonical inclusions:
\[ \xymatrix{ 0\ar[r]&\widetilde X\ar[r]\ar[d]&Y'\ar[r]\ar[d]&Z'\ar[r]\ar[d]&0\\ 0\ar[r]&X\ar[r]^u&Y\ar[r]^v&Z\ar[r]&0. } \]

It shows that the morphism \(\widetilde X\longrightarrow X\) is an AR-isomorphism, which allows us to reduce to the case where \(Y\) and \(Z\) are strict. In this case, since \(Z\) is strict and AR-\(J\)-adic, it follows from (3.2.3) that there exists an integer \(r\) such that \(\ell_r(Z)\) is \(J\)-adic. Consideration of the commutative exact diagram
\[ \xymatrix{ 0\ar[r]&\widetilde X\ar[r]\ar[d]&\ell_r(Y)\ar[r]\ar[d]&\ell_r(Z)\ar[r]\ar[d]&0\\ 0\ar[r]&X\ar[r]^u&Y\ar[r]^v&Z\ar[r]&0, } \]
in which as before \(\widetilde X\longrightarrow X\) is an AR-isomorphism, allows us to reduce to the case where \(Y\) is strict and where \(Z\) is \(J\)-adic. Then
(3.1.3 (ii)) allows us to conclude.
4. Filtrations and gradings.
4.1. Generalities.
4.1.1. Let \(C\) be an abelian category and \(X=(X_n)_{n\in\mathbb Z}\) a projective system indexed by \(\mathbb Z\) with values in \(C\). We assume
For every element \(p\) of \(\mathbb Z\), we define a subobject \(F^pX\) of \(X\) in the category \(P_1=\operatorname{Hom}(\mathbb Z^o,C)\) by setting:
\[ (F^pX)_n=F^pX_n= \begin{cases} \operatorname{Ker}(X_n\longrightarrow X_{p-1})&\text{if }n\geq p-1,\\ 0&\text{if }n<p-1, \end{cases} \]
the transition morphisms being defined in the obvious way. We thus obtain a decreasing filtration \((F^pX)_{p\in\mathbb Z}\) of \(X\) satisfying:
\(a\) \(F^pX=X\quad\) if \(p\leq0\),
\(b\) \((F^pX)_{p-1}=0\quad\) for every \(p\).
Moreover, the snake lemma applied to the commutative exact diagram \(D\) below proves that as soon as \(n\geq p\), the canonical morphism:
\[ F^pX_n/F^{p+1}X_n\longrightarrow \operatorname{Im}(X_n\longrightarrow X_p)\cap \operatorname{Ker}(X_p\longrightarrow X_{p-1}) \]
is an isomorphism.

\[ \xymatrix{ 0\ar[r]&{\operatorname{Ker}(X_n\longrightarrow X_p)}\ar[r]\ar[d]&X_n\ar[r]\ar[d]& {\operatorname{Im}(X_n\longrightarrow X_p)}\ar[r]\ar[d]&0\\ 0\ar[r]&0\ar[r]&X_{p-1}\ar[r]^{\sim}&X_{p-1}\ar[r]&0 }. \]

If now \(X\) is a projective system indexed by \(\mathbb N\) with values in \(C\), we extend it to a projective system indexed by \(\mathbb Z\) by setting \(X_n=0\) (\(n<0\)), with the obvious transition morphisms, which allows us to apply to it the preceding constructions.
This allows us to consider also the category
\[ P=\operatorname{Hom}(\mathbb N^o,C) \]
as a full subcategory of \(P_1\).

blocks.title: DEFINITION
blocks.html: DEFINITION 4.1.2. — Let \(X\) be a projective system indexed by \(\mathbb Z\). We say that \(X\) is essentially constant if there exists an integer \(p\) such that, for every pair \((r,s)\) of integers greater than or equal to \(p\) and satisfying the inequality \(s\geq r\), the transition morphism \[ X_s\longrightarrow X_r \] is an isomorphism.
It is clear that it suffices to require this when \(r=p\).

blocks.title: PROPOSITION
blocks.html:
PROPOSITION 4.1.3. (Property (MLAR) and graded objects). The following assertions for an object \(X\) of the category \(P\) are equivalent:

(i) \(X\) satisfies (MLAR).

(ii) The projective system with values in the category of objects graded by \(\mathbb N\) of \(C\):

\[ r\longmapsto(\operatorname{gr}^{\alpha}(X_{\alpha+r}))_{\alpha\in\mathbb N}, \]

where the transition morphisms are obvious, is essentially constant.

(iii) The projective system with values in the category of objects graded by \(\mathbb N\) of \(C\):

\[ n\longmapsto(\operatorname{gr}^{p}(X_n))_{p\in\mathbb N}, \]

where the transition morphisms are obvious, satisfies (MLAR).
Proof: (i) \(\Rightarrow\) (ii): follows from the expression for \(F^pX_n/F^{p+1}X_n\) given in (4.1.1).
(ii) \(\Rightarrow\) (iii): Let \(r\) be an integer such that for every \(s\geq r\) and every \(p\) the canonical morphism
\[ \operatorname{gr}^{p}(X_{p+s})\longrightarrow \operatorname{gr}^{p}(X_{p+r}) \]
is an isomorphism. We are going to see that for every pair \((m,n)\) of integers with \(m\geq n+r\), the equality below holds for every integer \(p\):
\[ \operatorname{Im}(\operatorname{gr}^{p}(X_m)\longrightarrow \operatorname{gr}^{p}(X_n))= \operatorname{Im}(\operatorname{gr}^{p}(X_{n+r})\longrightarrow \operatorname{gr}^{p}(X_n)). \]
Indeed:
\(a\) If \(p\geq n+1\), \(\operatorname{gr}^{p}(X_n)=0\),
\(b\) if \(p\leq n\), the chain of inequalities \(m\geq n+r\geq p+r\) shows that the canonical morphism
\[ \operatorname{gr}^{p}(X_m)\longrightarrow \operatorname{gr}^{p}(X_{n+r}) \]
is an isomorphism, whence the assertion.
(iii) \(\Rightarrow\) (i): Denoting by \(Y\) the projective system of (iii), let \(r\) be an integer such that:
\[ \operatorname{Im}(Y[s]\longrightarrow Y)= \operatorname{Im}(Y[r]\longrightarrow Y)\qquad(s\geq r). \]

If \(n\) and \(\ell\) are two integers, with \(\ell\geq n\), the filtration induced on \(\operatorname{Im}(X_\ell\longrightarrow X_n)\) by that of \(X_n\) is also the "quotient" filtration of that of \(X_\ell\) and consequently, equipping \(\operatorname{Im}(X_\ell\longrightarrow X_n)\) with this filtration, the equality below holds:
\[ \operatorname{gr}^{p}(\operatorname{Im}(X_\ell\longrightarrow X_n))= \operatorname{Im}(\operatorname{gr}^{p}(X_\ell)\longrightarrow \operatorname{gr}^{p}(X_n)). \]
In particular, condition (iii) can be expressed by saying that as soon as \(\ell\geq n+r\), the canonical monomorphism
\[ j:\operatorname{Im}(X_\ell\longrightarrow X_n)\longrightarrow \operatorname{Im}(X_{n+r}\longrightarrow X_n) \]
induces an isomorphism on the graded objects. The gradings being finite, we deduce that \(j\) itself is an isomorphism, whence (i).
4.1.4. If \(X\) is an object of \(P\) satisfying (ML), it follows from the expression for the graded object recalled in (4.1.1) that for every integer \(p\) the projective system:
\[ \operatorname{gr}^{p}(X)=\operatorname{gr}^{p}(X_n)_{n\in\mathbb N} \]
is essentially constant.
Its projective limit (which exists without any hypothesis on the abelian category \(C\)) will be called the \(p\)-th component of the strict graded object of \(X\) and denoted:
\[ \operatorname{grs}^{p}(X). \]
4.2. Projective systems adapted to \(J\) and the Artin-Rees lemma.
We assume given a commutative ring \(A\), an ideal \(J\) of \(A\) and an abelian \(A\)-category.

blocks.title: DEFINITION
blocks.html: DEFINITION 4.2.1. — An object \(X\) of the category \(P=\operatorname{Hom}(\mathbb N^o,C)\) is said to be adapted to \(J\) if its projective-system filtration \((F^pX)_{p\in\mathbb N}\) (4.1.1) is compatible with the \(J\)-adic filtration of \(X\), i.e. if for every pair \((p,q)\) of nonnegative integers the inclusion below holds: \[ J^pF^q(X)\subset F^{p+q}(X). \]

blocks.title: LEMMA
blocks.html:
LEMMA 4.2.2. — Let \(X\) be an object of the category \(P\). The following assertions are equivalent:

(i) \(X\) is adapted to \(J\).

(ii) For every pair \((n,p)\) of integers with \(p\leq n+1\), we have

\[ J^pF^{n-p+1}(X_n)=0. \]
Proof:
(i) \(\Rightarrow\) (ii): then \(J^pF^{n-p+1}(X_n)\subset F^{n+1}(X_n)=0\).
(ii) \(\Rightarrow\) (i): If \(n<p+q\), \(F^{p+q}(X_n)=0\) by definition.
Moreover, \(J^pF^q(X_n)\) is contained in \(J^{n+1-q}F^q(X_n)\) and the latter is zero by hypothesis.
If \(n\geq p+q\), the image of \(J^pF^q(X_n)\) in \(X_{p+q-1}\) under the transition morphism is contained in \(J^pF^q(X_{p+q-1})\), which is equal to zero by hypothesis. The inclusion
\[ J^pF^q(X_n)\subset F^{p+q}(X_n) \]
follows by definition of the projective-system filtration.
It follows in particular from (4.2.2) that every projective subsystem of a projective system adapted to \(J\) is adapted to \(J\).
Examples:
a) A \(J\)-adic projective system is adapted to \(J\). It follows that every projective subsystem of a \(J\)-adic projective system is adapted to \(J\).
b) If a projective system \(X\) satisfies condition (ML) and is adapted to \(J\), its projective system of universal images is also adapted to \(J\).
4.2.3. Graded object of a projective system adapted to \(J\).
Let \(X\) be a projective system adapted to \(J\). The filtration of \(X\) being compatible with \(J\), it is clear that the graded object
\[ \operatorname{gr}^p(X)_{p\in\mathbb N} \]
is canonically equipped with a structure of graded \(\operatorname{gr}_J(A)\)-module. In particular, we deduce by passage to the limit that when \(X\) satisfies condition (ML), the graded object
\[ \operatorname{grs}(X)=(\operatorname{grs}^p(X))_{p\in\mathbb N} \]
is canonically equipped with a structure of graded \(\operatorname{gr}_J(A)\)-module.
Let us explain briefly how, when moreover \(X\) is strict, we define the corresponding morphisms:
\[ \omega_{pq}:J^p/J^{p+1}\otimes_A F^q(X_q)\longrightarrow F^{p+q}(X_{p+q}). \]
The projective system \(X\) being strict, we know (4.1.1 and 4.1.2) that the canonical morphism below is an isomorphism:
\[ \varphi:F^q(X_{p+q})/F^{q+1}(X_{p+q})\longrightarrow F^q(X_q). \]
Moreover, the fact that the filtration of \(X\) is adapted to \(J\) allows us to exhibit a morphism
\[ J^p\otimes_A F^q(X_{p+q})\longrightarrow F^{p+q}(X_{p+q}) \]
which factors, given that \(J^{p+1}F^q(X_{p+q})\subset F^{p+q+1}(X_{p+q})=0\), and \(J^pF^{q+1}(X_{p+q})\subset F^{p+q+1}(X_{p+q})=0\), through
into a morphism
\[ \beta:(J^p/J^{p+1})\otimes_A(F^q(X_{p+q})/F^{q+1}(X_{p+q})) \longrightarrow F^{p+q}(X_{p+q}). \]
The morphism \(\omega_{pq}\) is then given by the following expression:
\[ \omega_{pq}=\beta\circ(\operatorname{id}_{J^p/J^{p+1}}\otimes_A\varphi)^{-1}. \]

blocks.title: LEMMA
blocks.html: LEMMA 4.2.4. — Let \(X\) be a \(J\)-adic projective system. The canonical morphism, \[ \operatorname{gr}_J(A)\otimes_A X_0\longrightarrow\operatorname{grs}(X), \] coming from the graded \(\operatorname{gr}_J(A)\)-module structure of \(\operatorname{grs}(X)\), is an epimorphism.
Proof: Follows immediately from the explanation of the morphisms \(\omega_{pq}\) given in (4.2.3).

blocks.title: PROPOSITION
blocks.html:
PROPOSITION 4.2.5. (Characterization of strict AR-\(J\)-adic projective systems by means of the associated graded objects).

Let \(X\) be a strict projective system indexed by \(\mathbb N\) with values in \(C\). We assume that for every integer \(n\), we have:

\[ J^{n+1}X_n=0. \]

The following assertions are equivalent:

(i) \(X\) is AR-\(J\)-adic.

(ii) There exists an integer \(r>0\) such that for every integer \(n\) the inclusion below holds:

\[ F^n(X_n)\subset J^{n-r}X_n. \]

(iii) There exists an integer \(r\) such that, for every integer \(m\), the canonical morphism below is an isomorphism as soon as \(n\geq m+r\):

\[ \operatorname{gr}^n(J^mX)\longrightarrow\operatorname{gr}^n(X). \]

In the statement of (ii), we agree to set \(J^{n-r}X_n=X_n\) for \(n<r\).

Proof: The equivalence of (ii) and (iii) is clear, since \(X\) and \(J^mX\) are strict. Let us prove that of (i) and (ii). We know (3.2.3) that (i) is equivalent to the existence of an integer \(r\) such that for every pair \((m,n)\) of integers with \(m\geq n+r\), the transition morphism below is an isomorphism:
\[ X_{m+1}/J^{n+1}X_{m+1}\longrightarrow X_m/J^{n+1}X_m. \]
Consider then the commutative exact diagram below.

\[ \xymatrix{ &0\ar[d]&0\ar[d]&\\ &J^{n+1}X_{m+1}\ar[r]\ar[d]&J^{n+1}X_m\ar[r]\ar[d]&0\\ 0\ar[r]&F^{m+1}(X_{m+1})\ar[r]&X_{m+1}\ar[r]\ar[d]&X_m\ar[r]\ar[d]&0\\ &&X_{m+1}/J^{n+1}X_{m+1}\ar[r]\ar[d]&X_m/J^{n+1}X_m\ar[r]\ar[d]&0\\ &&0&0 } \]
It shows the existence of a canonical isomorphism between
\[ \ker(X_{m+1}/J^{n+1}X_{m+1}\longrightarrow X_m/J^{n+1}X_m) \]
and

In particular, for the transition morphism after reduction modulo \(J^{n+1}\) to be an isomorphism, it is necessary and sufficient that \(F^{m+1}(X_{m+1})\) be contained in \(J^{n+1}X_{m+1}\), which is precisely assertion (ii).

blocks.title: THEOREM
blocks.html:
THEOREM 4.2.6. (Artin-Rees lemma). — Let \(X\) be a projective system indexed by \(\mathbb N\) with values in \(C\). We assume that:

(i) \(X\) satisfies (MLAR),

(ii) \(X\) is adapted to \(J\),

(iii) \(\operatorname{grs}(X)\) is a graded \((\operatorname{gr}_J(A),C)\)-module of finite type, i.e. for every increasing sequence \((M_n)_{n\in\mathbb N}\) of graded sub-\(\operatorname{gr}_J(A)\)-modules of \(\operatorname{grs}(X)\) of which \(\operatorname{grs}(X)\) is the union, there exists an integer \(p\) such that

\[ \operatorname{grs}(X)=M_p. \]

Then \(X\) is AR-\(J\)-adic.
Proof: Recall that if \(X'\) is the projective system of universal images of \(X\), we have set:
\[ \operatorname{grs}(X)=\operatorname{gr}(X'). \]
Moreover, \(X'\) is adapted to \(J\), so that we are reduced to showing that \(X'\) is AR-\(J\)-adic.
Suppose the following lemma has been proved:

blocks.title: LEMMA
blocks.html: LEMMA 4.2.7. — Let \(X\) be a strict projective system adapted to \(J\). For every integer \(s\), the graded subobject \(((M_s)_n)_{n\in\mathbb N}\) of \(\operatorname{grs}(X)\) defined by the equalities below: \[ (M_s)_n=J^{n-s}X_n\cap F^n(X_n)\qquad(J^i=A\text{ if }i<0), \] is a graded sub-\(\operatorname{gr}_J(A)\)-module of \(\operatorname{grs}(X)\).

To prove the theorem, we may assume \(X\) strict. Then \(\operatorname{grs}(X)\) is the union of the increasing sequence of the \(M_s\), hence equal to one of them. This means that there exists an integer \(s_0\) such that
\[ F^n(X_n)\subset J^{n-s_0}X_n \]
for every integer \(n\). We conclude by (4.2.5).
Proof of 4.2.7. Let us take up again the notations of (4.2.3). It is a matter of seeing that the morphism
\[ \omega_{pq}:J^p/J^{p+1}\otimes_A F^q(X_q)\longrightarrow F^{p+q}(X_{p+q}) \]
sends \(J^p/J^{p+1}\otimes_A(J^{q-s}X_q\cap F^q(X_q))\) into \(J^{p+q-s}X_{p+q}\cap F^{p+q}(X_{p+q})\).
Now, the canonical morphism
\[ \rho:X_{p+q}/F^{q+1}(X_{p+q})\longrightarrow X_q \]
is an isomorphism and sends \((J^{q-s}X_{p+q}+F^{q+1}(X_{p+q}))/F^{q+1}(X_{p+q})\) onto \(J^{q-s}X_q\).
We deduce that the inverse image under \(\operatorname{id}(J^p/J^{p+1})\otimes_A\varphi\) (4.2.3) of
\[ J^p/J^{p+1}\otimes_A(J^{q-s}X_q\cap F^q(X_q)) \]
is
It is now clear that the image under \(\beta\) (4.2.3) of this last object is contained in \(J^{p+q-s}X_{p+q}\cap J^pF^q(X_{p+q})\), hence a fortiori in
Whence the lemma.
5. Noetherian \(J\)-adic and AR-\(J\)-adic projective systems.
Throughout this paragraph, we assume given a commutative noetherian ring \(A\), an ideal \(J\) of \(A\) and an abelian \(A\)-category \(C\).
5.1. Generalities.

blocks.title: DEFINITION
blocks.html: DEFINITION 5.1.1. — An object \(X\) of the category \(\operatorname{Hom}(\mathbb N^\circ,C)\) is said to be \(J\)-adic noetherian (resp. artinian) if it is \(J\)-adic and if its components are noetherian (resp. artinian) objects of the category \(C\).

We denote by \(J\text{-}\operatorname{adn}(C)\) (resp. \(J\text{-}\operatorname{adf}(C)\)) the full subcategory of \(P=\operatorname{Hom}(\mathbb N^\circ,C)\) whose objects are the noetherian (resp. artinian) \(J\)-adic projective systems. It is clear (3.1.3) that an extension of noetherian (resp. artinian) \(J\)-adic projective systems is of the same type.

blocks.title: PROPOSITION
blocks.html: PROPOSITION 5.1.2. — Let \(X\) be an \(J\)-adic object of \(P\). For \(X\) to be \(J\)-adic noetherian (resp. artinian), it is necessary and sufficient that it be \(J\)-adic and that \(X_0\) be noetherian (resp. artinian).
Indeed, let \(X\) be an \(J\)-adic projective system such that \(X_0\) is noetherian (resp. artinian). Consider then the canonical epimorphism (4.2.4):
\[ \operatorname{gr}_J(A)\otimes_A X_0\longrightarrow \operatorname{grs}(X). \]
Since \(X_0\) is noetherian (resp. artinian) and \(J^n\) of finite type for every \(n\), the existence of this epimorphism shows that for every integer \(n\), the kernel of the canonical morphism
\[ X_n\longrightarrow X_{n-1} \]
is noetherian (resp. artinian). We deduce at once by induction on \(n\) that the \(X_n\) are noetherian (resp. artinian).

blocks.title: DEFINITION
blocks.html: DEFINITION 5.1.3. — Let \(X\) be a projective system indexed by \(\mathbb N\) with values in \(C\). We say that it is AR-\(J\)-adic noetherian (resp. artinian) if it is AR-\(J\)-adic, and if its components are noetherian (resp. artinian) objects.

We denote by \(\operatorname{AR}\text{-}J\text{-}\operatorname{adn}(C)\) (resp. \(\operatorname{AR}\text{-}J\text{-}\operatorname{adf}(C)\)) the full subcategory of \(\operatorname{Hom}_{\mathrm{AR}}(\mathbb N^\circ,C)\) (2.4) generated by the images of the noetherian (resp. artinian) AR-\(J\)-adic projective systems.
It is then clear that the canonical functors

\[ P_{\mathrm{AR}}^n:J\text{-}\operatorname{adn}(C)\longrightarrow \operatorname{AR}\text{-}J\text{-}\operatorname{adn}(C) \] \[ P_{\mathrm{AR}}^f:J\text{-}\operatorname{adf}(C)\longrightarrow \operatorname{AR}\text{-}J\text{-}\operatorname{adf}(C) \]
obtained by restriction of \(P_{\mathrm{AR}}\) (2.4) are equivalences of categories.
Let us briefly recall the statement and proof of Hilbert's theorem:

blocks.title: THEOREM
blocks.html:
THEOREM 5.1.4. (Hilbert's theorem). — Let \(A\) be a commutative ring, \(C\) an abelian \(A\)-category, \(M\) an object of finite type (resp. noetherian) of \(C\).

For every \(A\)-algebra of finite type \(B\) graded by \(\mathbb N\), the graded \((B,C)\)-module \(B\otimes_A M\) is of finite type (resp. noetherian).

(N.B. If \(B=\bigoplus_{n\geq0}B_n\), \(B\otimes_A M\) denotes the graded object \((B_n\otimes_A M)_{n\in\mathbb N}\).)

Proof: We reduce immediately to the case where \(B\) is the polynomial ring (A[T]), equipped with its usual grading. The \((B,C)\)-module \(A[T]\otimes_A M=(M_n)_{n\in\mathbb N}\) is then obtained by setting \(M_n=M\) for every \(n\), with \(T\) sending, for every integer \(n\), \(M_n\) into \(M_{n+1}\) by the identity of \(M\).
Let \(N\) be a graded sub-\((A[T],C)\)-module of \(A[T]\otimes_A M\). The components \((N_n)_{n\in\mathbb N}\) of \(N\) define an increasing sequence:
\[ N_0\subset N_1\subset\cdots\subset N_n\subset\cdots \]
of subobjects of \(M\). Suppose now that \(M\) is noetherian (resp. of finite type) and let
\[ N^1\subset N^2\subset\cdots\subset N^p\subset\cdots \]
be an increasing sequence of graded sub-\((A[T],C)\)-modules of \(A[T]\otimes_A M\), which under the first hypothesis (when \(M\) is of finite type) has union \(A[T]\otimes_A M\).
Consider then the double-entry table below:

\[ \begin{array}{ccccccc} N_0^1&\subset&N_0^2&\subset&\cdots\subset N_0^p&\subset&\cdots\\ \cap&&\cap&&\cap\\ N_1^1&&\vdots&&\vdots\\ \vdots&&\vdots&&\vdots\\ \cap&&\cap&&\cap\\ N_n^1&\subset&N_n^2&\subset&\cdots\subset N_n^p&&\cdots\\ \cap&&\cap&&\cap \end{array} \]
It is clear by the hypothesis on \(M\) that there exists a pair \((p_0,n_0)\) such that \(N_{n_0}^{p_0}\) is maximal (resp. equal to \(M\)).

If \(p_1\) is an integer \(geq p_0\) such that the \(N_n^{p_1}\) \((n\leq n_0)\) are the largest elements of the sequences \((N_n^p)_{p\in\mathbb N}\), it is clear that \(N^p=N^{p_1}\) as soon as \(p\geq p_1\), which allows us to conclude in both cases.

blocks.title: COROLLARY
blocks.html: COROLLARY 5.1.5. — Suppose in addition that the category \(C\) possesses countable direct sums. Then for every object \(M\) of finite type (resp. noetherian) of \(C\) and every \(A\)-algebra of finite type \(B\), the \((B,C)\)-module \(B\otimes_A M\) is of finite type (resp. noetherian).

Proof: We reduce to the case where \(B=A[T]\). Let \(N\) be a sub-\((A[T],C)\)-module of \(A[T]\otimes_A M\) (considered as an object of \(C\)). We denote by \(\operatorname{gr}(N)\) the graded object of \(N\) for the filtration induced by the natural filtration of graded object of \(A[T]\otimes_A M\): \(\operatorname{gr}(N)\) is a graded sub-\((A[T],C)\)-module of the graded \((A[T],C)\)-module \(A[T]\otimes_A M\).
Now let
\[ N^0\subset N^1\subset\cdots\subset N^p\subset\cdots \]

be an increasing sequence of sub-\((A[T],C)\)-modules of \(A[T]\otimes_A M\), with union \(A[T]\otimes_A M\) under the first hypothesis. Under the first hypothesis (\(M\) of finite type), there exists an integer (i) such that \(\operatorname{gr}(N^i)=A[T]\otimes_A M\), whence \(N^i=A[T]\otimes_A M\). Under the second hypothesis \((M) noethérien\), the sequence of the \(\operatorname{gr}(N^i)\) is stationary, hence the sequence of the \(N^i\) as well.
In what follows, Hilbert's theorem will be useful to us mainly through the following proposition.

blocks.title: PROPOSITION
blocks.html: PROPOSITION 5.1.6. — Let \(A\) be a commutative noetherian ring, \(J\) an ideal of \(A\), \(C\) an abelian \(A\)-category. For every noetherian \(J\)-adic projective system (5.1.1) \(X\), the graded object \(\operatorname{grs}(X)\) is a noetherian \((\operatorname{gr}_J(A),C)\)-module.

Proof: Since \(\operatorname{gr}_J(A)\) is an \(A\)-algebra of finite type, it follows from Hilbert's theorem (5.1.4) that \(\operatorname{gr}_J(A)\otimes_A X_0\) is a noetherian graded \((\operatorname{gr}_J(A),C)\)-module. The assertion then follows from the existence of the epimorphism (4.2.4):
\[ \operatorname{gr}_J(A)\otimes_A X_0\longrightarrow \operatorname{grs}(X). \]
5.2. Structure of \(J\text{-}\operatorname{adn}(C)\) and \(\operatorname{AR}\text{-}J\text{-}\operatorname{adn}(C)\).

In this section, we shall denote by \(\mathcal E_C\), or \(\mathcal E\) if no confusion is possible, the full subcategory of \(\operatorname{Hom}(\mathbb N^\circ,C)\) whose objects are the noetherian AR-\(J\)-adic projective systems.

blocks.title: PROPOSITION
blocks.html: PROPOSITION 5.2.1. — The category \(\mathcal E_C\) is stable under kernels and cokernels in \(\operatorname{Hom}(\mathbb N^\circ,C)\). In other words, \(\mathcal E_C\) is an abelian category and the inclusion functor: \[ \mathcal E_C\longrightarrow \operatorname{Hom}(\mathbb N^\circ,C) \] is exact.

Proof: Let \(u:X\longrightarrow Y\) be a morphism of \(\mathcal E_C\). We shall see successively that the cokernel, the image and the kernel of \(u\) are AR-\(J\)-adic noetherian. (Of course the proof of the fact that the image of \(u\) is AR-\(J\)-adic noetherian is only a technical intermediate step.)
a) \(\operatorname{Coker}(u)\) is AR-\(J\)-adic noetherian. Since \(X\) satisfies (MLAR) and \(Y\) is AR-\(J\)-adic, \(\operatorname{Coker}(u)\) is AR-\(J\)-adic (3.2.4 (i)).
b) \(\operatorname{Im}(u)\) is AR-\(J\)-adic noetherian.
Thanks to the exact sequence:
\[ 0\longrightarrow \operatorname{Im}(u)\longrightarrow Y\longrightarrow \operatorname{Coker}(u)\longrightarrow0, \]
the assertion will follow from lemma 5.2.1.1. below.

blocks.title: LEMMA
blocks.html: LEMMA 5.2.1.1. — Let \[ 0\longrightarrow L\longrightarrow M\longrightarrow N\longrightarrow0 \] be an exact sequence in the category \(\operatorname{Hom}(\mathbb N^\circ,C)\). If \(M\) and \(N\) are AR-\(J\)-adic noetherian, \(L\) is AR-\(J\)-adic noetherian.

It obviously suffices to see that it is AR-\(J\)-adic. We reduce, in the same way as in the proof of (3.2.4), to the case where \(M\) and \(N\) are strict, and even \(J\)-adic noetherian. In this case, it follows from 3.1.3 (ii) that \(L\) is strict. The projective system \(L\) is strict, adapted to \(J\), and its graded object, as a graded subobject of \(\operatorname{grs}(M)\), is noetherian: \(\operatorname{grs}(M)\), a quotient of \(\operatorname{gr}_J(A)\otimes_A M_0\), is noetherian (5.1.6). The assertion then follows from the Artin-Rees lemma (4.2.6).
c) \(\operatorname{Ker}(u)\) is AR-\(J\)-adic noetherian. It suffices to apply (5.2.1.1) to the obvious exact sequence below:
\[ 0\longrightarrow \operatorname{Ker}(u)\longrightarrow X\longrightarrow \operatorname{Im}(u)\longrightarrow0. \]

blocks.title: PROPOSITION
blocks.html: PROPOSITION 5.2.2. — The objects of the abelian category \(AR\text{-}J\text{-}\operatorname{adn}(C)\) are noetherian.

Proof: We reduce immediately to seeing that if we have a noetherian \(J\)-adic object \(X\), a sequence of noetherian \(J\)-adic objects \((Y^n)_{n\in\mathbb N}\) and morphisms of \(\operatorname{Hom}(\mathbb N^\circ,C)\)
\[ v_n:Y^n\longrightarrow X \]
such that
(i) the \(v_n\) become monomorphisms in \(AR\text{-}J\text{-}\operatorname{adn}(C)\),
(ii) for every integer \(n\), \(v_n\) factors through \(v_{n+1}\) in \(AR\text{-}J\text{-}\operatorname{adn}(C)\),
then the \(v_n\) remain stationary, up to isomorphism, for \(n\) large enough, in \(AR\text{-}J\text{-}\operatorname{adn}(C)\).
Now, to say that \(v_n\) factors through \(v_{n+1}\) in \(AR\text{-}J\text{-}\operatorname{adn}(C)\) means that there exist an integer \(r\) and a morphism
\[ f:Y^n[r]\longrightarrow Y^{n+1} \]
such that, \(a\) denoting the canonical morphism \(Y^n[r]\longrightarrow Y^n\), the equality below holds:
\[ v_n\circ a=v_{n+1}\circ f. \]

Since the morphism \(a\) is strict, we deduce in particular that the image of \(v_n\) is contained in that of \(v_{n+1}\). Replacing if necessary each \(Y^n\) by \(\operatorname{Im}(v_n)\), we are reduced to seeing that every increasing sequence of strict projective subsystems of \(X\) is stationary. Now, if \((Z^n)_{n\in\mathbb N}\) is such a sequence, since \(\operatorname{grs}(X)\) is noetherian, the increasing sequence of the \(\operatorname{grs}(Z^n)\) is stationary, hence so is that of the \(Z^n\).
Let us summarize:

blocks.title: THEOREM
blocks.html: THEOREM 5.2.3. — The categories \(J\text{-}\operatorname{adn}(C)\) and \(AR\text{-}J\text{-}\operatorname{adn}(C)\) are abelian and noetherian.
As regards \(AR\text{-}J\text{-}\operatorname{adn}(C)\), this is what we have just seen. As for \(J\text{-}\operatorname{adn}(C)\), it is equivalent to it.

blocks.title: PROPOSITION
blocks.html:
PROPOSITION 5.2.4. (stability under extension). Let \[ 0\longrightarrow X\longrightarrow Y\longrightarrow Z\longrightarrow0 \] be an exact sequence of \(\operatorname{Hom}(\mathbb N^\circ,C)\), with \(J^{n+1}Y_n=0\) for every \(n\). We assume that:

(i) \(Z\) is \(AR\text{-}J\)-adic,

(ii) \(X\) is \(AR\text{-}J\)-adic artinian.

Then \(Y\) is \(AR\text{-}J\)-adic.

Proof: We reduce as in the proof of (3.2.4) to the case where \(Y\) and \(Z\) are strict and \(Z\) is \(J\)-adic. Then (3.1.3 (ii)) \(X\) is strict. There then exists an integer \(r\) such that the projective system \((X_{n+r}/J^{n+1}X_{n+r})_{n\in\mathbb N}\) is \(J\)-adic artinian. Since \(X\) is a quotient of it and is \(AR\)-isomorphic to it, we are reduced to proving the following lemma:

blocks.title: LEMMA
blocks.html: LEMMA 5.2.4.1. — Let \[ 0\longrightarrow T\longrightarrow X\longrightarrow Y\longrightarrow Z \longrightarrow0 \] be an exact sequence of \(\operatorname{Hom}(\mathbb N^\circ,C)\), with \(J^{n+1}Y_n=0\) for every \(n\). If \(X\) is \(J\)-adic artinian, \(Z\) is \(J\)-adic and \(T\) is \(AR\)-null, then \(Y\) is \(AR\text{-}J\)-adic.

To prove the lemma, we shall first see that, for every integer \(n\), the obvious projective system \((Y_{n+k}/J^{n+1}Y_{n+k})_{k\in\mathbb N}\) is essentially constant. Denoting by \(S_n^k\) the kernel of the obvious morphism
\[ X_{n+k}/J^{n+1}X_{n+k}\longrightarrow Y_{n+k}/J^{n+1}Y_{n+k}, \]
consider the obvious commutative exact diagram below:

\[ \xymatrix{ 0\ar[r]&S_n^k\ar[r]\ar[d]&X_{n+k}/J^{n+1}X_{n+k}\ar[r]\ar[d]^{\sim}& Y_{n+k}/J^{n+1}Y_{n+k}\ar[r]\ar[d]&Z_{n+k}/J^{n+1}Z_{n+k}\ar[r]\ar[d]^{\sim}&0\\ 0\ar[r]&T_n\ar[r]&X_n\ar[r]&Y_n\ar[r]&Z_n\ar[r]&0. } \]

The integer \(n\) being fixed, the objects \(S_n^k\) identify with subobjects of \(T_n\) and form a decreasing sequence. Since \(T_n\) is artinian, there therefore exists an integer \(k_0\) such that \(S_n^k=S_n^{k_0}\) for every integer \(k\geq k_0\), whence it follows at once that the canonical morphisms
\[ Y_{n+k}/J^{n+1}Y_{n+k}\longrightarrow Y_{n+k_0}/J^{n+1}Y_{n+k_0} \]
are isomorphisms.
This said, let us denote by \(Y'\) the projective system defined by
\[ Y'_n=\varprojlim_r Y_{n+r}/J^{n+1}Y_{n+r} \]
for the objects, and in the obvious way for the morphisms. The projective system \(Y'\) is \(J\)-adic. Moreover, we obtain "by passage to the limit" a commutative exact diagram of the type below:
\[ \xymatrix{ 0\ar[r]&S\ar[r]\ar[d]&X\ar[r]\ar[d]^{\mathrm{id}}&Y'\ar[r]\ar[d]&Z\ar[r]\ar[d]^{\mathrm{id}}&0\\ 0\ar[r]&T\ar[r]&X\ar[r]&Y\ar[r]&Z\ar[r]&0. } \]

Since \(T\) is \(AR\)-null, the snake lemma applied to this diagram shows that the obvious morphism \(Y'\longrightarrow Y\) is a \(AR\)-isomorphism, whence the fact that \(Y\) is \(AR\text{-}J\)-adic.

blocks.title: COROLLARY
blocks.html:
COROLLARY 5.2.5. — Let \[ 0\longrightarrow X\longrightarrow Y\longrightarrow Z\longrightarrow0 \] be an exact sequence of \(\operatorname{Hom}(\mathbb N^\circ,C)\), with \(J^{n+1}Y_n=0\) for every \(n\). We assume that:

(i) \(X\) is \(AR\text{-}J\)-adic artinian (resp. artinian and noetherian),

(ii) \(Z\) is \(AR\text{-}J\)-adic artinian (resp. noetherian).

Then \(Y\) is \(AR\text{-}J\)-adic artinian (resp. noetherian).
5.3. Noetherian \(AR\text{-}J\)-adic projective systems and \(\partial\)-functors.
Let \(C\) and \(D\) be two abelian \(A\)-categories and \(F:C\longrightarrow D\) an additive functor. The functor \(F\) extends in a clear way to a functor, still denoted \(F\),
\[ F:\operatorname{Hom}(\mathbb N^\circ,C)\longrightarrow \operatorname{Hom}(\mathbb N^\circ,D), \]
which is additive and "commutes with translations".

blocks.title: PROPOSITION
blocks.html:
PROPOSITION 5.3.1. — Let \(C\) and \(D\) be two abelian \(A\)-categories and \[ T=(T^i)_{i\in\mathbb N} \] an exact \(\partial\)-functor, \(A\)-linear, from \(C\) to \(D\). We assume that:

(i) For every noetherian object \(M\) of \(C\) annihilated by a power of \(J\) and every integer \(n\), \(T^n(M)\) is noetherian.

(ii) For every graded \(A\)-algebra of finite type \(B\), annihilated by \(J\), and every noetherian graded \((B,C)\)-module \(N\), the graded \((B,D)\)-modules \(T^n(N)\) (\(n\in\mathbb N\)) are noetherian.

Then for every noetherian \(AR\text{-}J\)-adic object \(X\) of \(\operatorname{Hom}(\mathbb N^\circ,C)\), the \(T^n(X)\) are \(AR\text{-}J\)-adic noetherian.

Proof: Since the functors \(T^n\) commute with translations, it suffices to show the assertion when \(X\) is \(J\)-adic noetherian. In this case, since \(\operatorname{grs}(X)\) is a noetherian graded \(\operatorname{gr}_J(A)\)-module (5.1.6), it follows from hypothesis (ii) and from a variant of a corollary of the theorem of SHIH (cf. Appendix A.3) that for every integer \(n\) the following assertions are satisfied:
\(a\) \(T^n(X)\) satisfies (MLAR),
\(b\) \(\operatorname{grs}T^n(X)\) is a noetherian graded \((\operatorname{gr}_J(A),D)\)-module.
The "Artin-Rees lemma" (4.2.6) allows us to conclude.

blocks.title: COROLLARY
blocks.html: COROLLARY 5.3.2. — The \(\partial\)-functor \[ T=(T^i)_{i\in\mathbb N}:\operatorname{Hom}(\mathbb N^\circ,C) \longrightarrow\operatorname{Hom}(\mathbb N^\circ,D) \] induces an exact \(\partial\)-functor, \(A\)-linear, \[ \mathcal E_C\longrightarrow\mathcal E_D, \] commuting with translations, hence by passage to the quotient a new exact \(\partial\)-functor, \(A\)-linear, still denoted \((T^i)_{i\in\mathbb N}\), \[ AR\text{-}J\text{-}\operatorname{adn}(C)\longrightarrow AR\text{-}J\text{-}\operatorname{adn}(D). \]
Appendix: The theorem of Shih.

The aim of the present appendix is to give a proof of the theorem of Shih in the form used in the proof of (5.3.1). This form being more general than the one appearing in EGA \(0_{\mathrm{III}}\), a direct proof, adapted from that of loc. cit., proved necessary.

Terminology: Let \(r_0\) be an integer \(\geq1\). The data, for every integer \(r\geq r_0\), of a bigraded object \(E_r=(E_r^{pq})_{p\in\mathbb Z,q\in\mathbb Z}\), equipped with a differential \(d_r\) of bidegree \((r,1-r)\), and for every \(r\) of an isomorphism
\[ H(E_r)\mathbin{\widetilde\longrightarrow}E_{r+1}, \]
will be called in what follows a spectral filter. One defines in the obvious way the notion of morphism of spectral filters.
A.1. Reminders on the spectral sequences associated with \(\partial\)-functors.

Let \(C\) be an abelian category and \(X\) an object of \(C\), equipped with a decreasing filtration \((F^pX)_{p\in\mathbb Z}\). For every exact \(\partial\)-functor from \(C\) to an abelian category \(A\), the so-called "exact couples" method (cf. for ex. MacLane, Homology, p. 336) allows us to describe a spectral sequence:
\[ E(X):\quad E_1^{pq}=T^{p+q}(F^pX/F^{p+1}X)\Rightarrow E^n=T^n(X), \]
for which the filtration of \(E^n\) is defined as follows:
\[ F^p(E^n)=\operatorname{Im}\bigl(T^n(F^pX)\longrightarrow T^n(X)\bigr) =\ker\bigl(T^n(X)\longrightarrow T^n(X/F^pX)\bigr). \]
Moreover, if the filtration of \(X\) is finite, the spectral sequence thus obtained is weakly convergent (EGA \(0_{\mathrm{III}}\) 11.1.3).
Functoriality. If \(f:X\longrightarrow Y\) is a morphism of filtered objects of \(C\), one constructs functorially a morphism of spectral sequences:
\[ E(f):E(X)\longrightarrow E(Y), \]

coinciding with \(T^{p+q}(\operatorname{gr}^pf)\) (resp. \(T^n(f)\)) on \(E_1^{pq}(X)\) (resp. \(E^n(X)\)). In particular, if \(u:X\longrightarrow X\) is an endomorphism of \(X\) such that \(u(F^pX)\subset F^{p+h}(X)\), for an integer \(h\) independent of \(p\), \(E(u)\) is an endomorphism of bidegree \((h,-h)\) of the spectral filter associated with \(E(X)\), commuting with the differentials.
A.2. The case of projective systems.
Now let \(X=(X_n)_{n\in\mathbb Z}\) be a projective system of objects of an abelian category \(C\), such that
If \(T\) denotes an exact \(\partial\)-functor from \(C\) to an abelian category \(A\), \(T\) extends naturally to an exact \(\partial\)-functor, still denoted \(T\):
\[ \operatorname{Hom}(\mathbb Z^\circ,C)\longrightarrow \operatorname{Hom}(\mathbb Z^\circ,A). \]
Whence, equipping \(X\) with its natural projective-system filtration (EGA \(0_{\mathrm{III}}\) 13.4.1), a spectral sequence:
\[ E(X)=\bigl(E(X_n)\bigr)_{n\in\mathbb Z}, \]
where the \(E(X_n)\) denote the spectral sequences associated with \(T\) and with the \(X_n\) equipped with the induced filtration.
Properties of \(E(X)\).
a) For every integer \(n\), the filtration of the abutment \(E^n(X)\) in the spectral sequence \(E(X)\) is identical to its natural projective-system filtration, when \(X\) is strict.
Indeed, denoting by the letter \(F\) (as in A 1) the first
and by the letter \(\Phi\) the second, we have for every integer \(\alpha\):

\[ F^pE^n(X)_\alpha =\ker\bigl(T^n(X_\alpha)\longrightarrow T^n(X_\alpha/F^pX_\alpha)\bigr) =\begin{cases} 0&\text{if }\alpha\leq p-1,\\ \ker\bigl(T^n(X_\alpha)\longrightarrow T^n(X_{p-1})\bigr) &\text{if }\alpha\geq p-1. \end{cases} \]
In both cases, we recognize the expression for \(\Phi^pE^n(X)\).
This shows in particular that, for \(\beta\geq\alpha\geq p\), the transition morphism

\[ \begin{array}{ccc} E_\infty^{pq}(X_\beta)&\longrightarrow&E_\infty^{pq}(X_\alpha)\\ \Vert&&\Vert\\ \operatorname{gr}^pT^{p+q}(X_\beta)&\longrightarrow& \operatorname{gr}^pT^{p+q}(X_\alpha) \end{array} \]
is a monomorphism, by EGA \(0_{\mathrm{III}}\) 13.4.1.3.
b) Fixing \(\alpha\in\mathbb Z\), it is clear (cf. the picture opposite) that, since \(F^i(X_\alpha)=0\) for \(i\geq\alpha+1\), we have:
\[ B_r^{pq}(X_\alpha)=B_{r+1}^{pq}(X_\alpha)\quad\text{for }r>p, \] \[ Z_r^{pq}(X_\alpha)=Z_{r+1}^{pq}(X_\alpha)\quad\text{for }r>\alpha-p. \]
\[ \begin{array}{c|c} q& (p,q)\\[-2pt] 1-r&\swarrow\!\!\!\longrightarrow^{\ r}\!\!\!\searrow\\ \hline 0&\phantom{(p,q)}\alpha\quad p \end{array} \]

c) When \(X\) satisfies the Mittag-Leffler condition, the various projective systems \(\operatorname{gr}^p(X)\) are essentially constant (EGA \(0_{\mathrm{III}}\) 13.4.3). It follows at once that, for \(r<+\infty\), the projective systems \(E_r^{pq}(X)\) are essentially constant. We denote by
\[ \mathcal E_r^{pq}(X) \]
their projective limits. The differential operators passing to the limit, we thus obtain a spectral filter \(\mathcal E_r^{pq}(X)\ (p,q\in\mathbb Z;\ r\geq1)\). The objects
\[ \mathcal Z_r^{pq}(X),\quad\mathcal B_r^{pq}(X)\qquad(r<+\infty) \]
associated with this spectral filter are precisely the limits of the essentially constant projective systems \(Z_r^{pq}(X)\) and \(B_r^{pq}(X)\).
From part b) it also follows that
which allows us to set
\[ \mathcal B_\infty^{pq}(X)=\mathcal B_{p+1}^{pq}(X). \]
Still assuming that \(X\) satisfies (ML), since, for every \(\alpha\), \(B_r^{pq}(X_\alpha)=B_{r+1}^{pq}(X_\alpha)\) as soon as \(r>p\) (cf. b)), we have a sequence of canonical monomorphisms:
\[ (S)\qquad\cdots\subset\mathcal E_r^{pq}(X)\subset\cdots\subset \mathcal E_{p+2}^{pq}(X)\subset\mathcal E_{p+1}^{pq}(X). \]
Assume from now on that \(X\) is strict. The following expressions for \(Z_r^{pq}(X_n)\) and \(B_r^{pq}(X_n)\):

\[ Z_r^{pq}(X_n)=\operatorname{Im}\left[ T^{p+q-1}(F^{p-r+1}X_n/F^pX_n)\mathbin{\mathop{\longrightarrow}^{\partial}} T^{p+q}(F^pX_n/F^{p+1}X_n)\right] \] \[ B_r^{pq}(X_n)=\operatorname{Im}\left[ T^{p+q}(F^pX_n/F^{p+r}X_n)\longrightarrow T^{p+q}(F^pX_n/F^{p+1}X_n)\right] \]
show that

\[ (d_1)\quad \left\{\begin{aligned} Z_r^{pq}(X_m)&\mathbin{\widetilde\longrightarrow}Z_r^{pq}(X_n) &&\text{for }m\geq n\geq p,\\ B_r^{pq}(X_m)&\mathbin{\widetilde\longrightarrow}B_r^{pq}(X_n) &&\text{for }m\geq n\geq p+r-1 \end{aligned}\right. \]
(the canonical morphisms are isomorphisms). In particular, the canonical morphism
\[ \mathcal E_r^{pq}(X)\longrightarrow E_r^{pq}(X_{p+r-1}) \]
is an isomorphism.
Moreover, it follows from b) that
\[ (d_2)\quad \left\{\begin{aligned} Z_\infty^{pq}(X_{p+r-1})&=Z_r^{pq}(X_{p+r-1})&&(r\geq1),\\ B_\infty^{pq}(X_{p+r-1})&=B_r^{pq}(X_{p+r-1})&&(r>p). \end{aligned}\right. \]
This allows us to define, for every \(r\), a canonical epimorphism
\[ \theta_r^{pq}:\mathcal E_r^{pq}(X)\longrightarrow E_\infty^{pq}(X_{p+r-1}) \]
which is an isomorphism when \(r>p\). Moreover, if \(a\) is an integer such that
\[ \mathcal B_r^{pq}(X)=\mathcal B_a^{pq}(X)\qquad(r\geq a), \]
(for example \(a=p+1\) works), so that

\[ (S\ \mathrm{bis})\qquad\cdots\lhook\joinrel\longrightarrow \mathcal E_r^{pq}(X)\lhook\joinrel\longrightarrow\cdots \lhook\joinrel\longrightarrow\mathcal E_{a+1}^{pq}(X) \lhook\joinrel\longrightarrow\mathcal E_a^{pq}(X), \]
then, for \(s\geq r\geq a\), the diagrams
\[ (d_3)\qquad \xymatrix{ \mathcal E_s^{pq}(X)\ar[r]^{\theta_s^{pq}}\ar[d]& E_\infty^{pq}(X_{p+s-1})\ar[d]^{\mathrm{can}}\\ \mathcal E_r^{pq}(X)\ar[r]_{\theta_r^{pq}}& E_\infty^{pq}(X_{p+r-1}) } \]
are commutative.
A.3. A variant of the theorem of Shih.
Suppose given a ring \(S\) filtered by ideals \(F^i(S)\ (i\geq0)\) and acting on \(X\) compatibly with its projective-system filtration.

From the generalities of A 1 it follows that for every integer \(i\), \(\operatorname{gr}^i(S)\) acts on the bigraded objects \(E_r(X)\) by morphisms of bidegree \((i,-i)\). In particular for every integer \(n\):
is canonically equipped with a structure of graded \((\operatorname{gr}(S),A)\)-module. The same holds by passage to the limit for:
\[ \mathcal E_1^{(n)}(X)=\bigl(\mathcal E_1^{p,q}(X)\bigr)_{p+q=n}. \]

The ring \(S\), acting on \(X\) compatibly with its projective-system filtration, likewise acts on \(T^n(X)\) for every integer \(n\). Consequently the strict graded object \(\operatorname{gr}_S T(X)\) is canonically equipped with a structure of graded \((\operatorname{gr}(S),C)\)-module, and likewise, when \(T^n(X)\) satisfies \((\mathrm{ML})\), the strict graded object \(\operatorname{gr}_S T^n(X)\) is canonically equipped with a structure of graded \((\operatorname{gr}(S),A)\)-module.

blocks.title: THEOREM A.3.
blocks.html:
THEOREM A.3. — Suppose that for some integer \(n\) the graded \((\operatorname{gr}(S),A)\)-modules
\[\mathcal E_1^{(n)}(X)=T^n(\operatorname{gr}_S(X))\quad\text{and}\quad \mathcal E_1^{(n-1)}(X)=T^{n-1}(\operatorname{gr}_S(X))\]

are noetherian. Then:

(i) \(T^n(X)\) satisfies \((\mathrm{MLAR})\),
(ii) \(\operatorname{gr}_S T^n(X)\) is a noetherian \((\operatorname{gr}(S),A)\)-module.
Proof:
i) For every pair \((m,r)\) of integers,
\[\mathcal B_r^{(m)}(X)=\bigl(\mathcal B_r^{p,m-p}(X)\bigr)_{p\in\mathbb N}\]

is a graded sub-\((\operatorname{gr}(S),A)\)-module of \(\mathcal E_1^{(m)}(X)\). In particular, for every integer \(m\), the \(\mathcal B_r^{(m)}(X)\) form an increasing sequence indexed by \(r\) of graded sub-\((\operatorname{gr}(S),A)\)-modules of \(\mathcal E_1^{(m)}(X)\). Using the fact that \(\mathcal E_1^{(n)}(X)\) and \(\mathcal E_1^{(n-1)}(X)\) are noetherian graded \((\operatorname{gr}(S),A)\)-modules, we deduce that the sequences
\[\bigl[\mathcal B_r^{(n)}(X)\bigr]_{r\in\mathbb N}\quad\text{and}\quad \bigl[\mathcal B_r^{(n-1)}(X)\bigr]_{r\in\mathbb N}\]
are stationary.
There therefore exists an integer \(a\geq1\) such that, for every \((p,q)\), with \(p+q=n\) or \(n-1\),
\[(\mathrm{A}.3.1)\qquad \mathcal B_r^{pq}(X)=\mathcal B_a^{pq}(X)\quad(r\geq a).\]
Consequently, for \(r\geq a+1\), the arrows \(d_r\) with source and target \(\mathcal E_r^{pq}(X)\) (\(p+q=n\)) are zero. In other words the monomorphisms
\[\mathcal E_s^{pq}(X)\lhook\joinrel\longrightarrow\mathcal E_r^{pq}(X)\qquad(s\geq r\geq a+1)\]
deduced from (A.3.1) with \(p+q=n\) are in fact isomorphisms.
For \(s\geq r\geq a+1\), the commutativity of the diagram \((d_3)\), with \(p+q=n\),
\[\xymatrix{\mathcal E_s^{pq}(X)\ar[r]^{\theta_s^{pq}}_{\text{epi}}\ar[d]_{\sim}&E_\infty^{pq}(X_{p+s-1})\ar[d]\\ \mathcal E_r^{pq}(X)\ar[r]^{\theta_r^{pq}}_{\text{epi}}&E_\infty^{pq}(X_{p+r-1})}\]
shows that the transition morphism
\[E_\infty^{pq}(X_{p+s-1})\longrightarrow E_\infty^{pq}(X_{p+r-1})\]
which is in any case a monomorphism (cf. a)) is an epimorphism, hence an isomorphism. In other words, the projective system
\[r\longmapsto\left[\bigoplus_p\operatorname{gr}_S^pT^n(X_{p+r})\right]_{r\geq1}\]
is constant from \(r=a\) on, hence \(T^n(X)\) satisfies \((\mathrm{MLAR})\) by (4.1.3).

ii) Denoting by \(\mathcal E_\infty^{(n)}(X)\), \(\mathcal Z_\infty^{(n)}(X)\), \(\mathcal B_\infty^{(n)}(X)\) the "limits" of the various projective systems indexed by \(r\) under consideration, we see that
\[\mathcal E_\infty^{(n)}(X)=\mathcal Z_\infty^{(n)}(X)/\mathcal B_\infty^{(n)}(X)\]
is a noetherian \((\operatorname{gr}(S),A)\)-module, since \(\mathcal Z_\infty^{(n)}(X)\) is contained in \(\mathcal E_1^{(n)}(X)\). Moreover it follows from A.2. a) that:
\[\mathcal E_\infty^{(n)}(X)=\operatorname{gr}_S T^n(X).\]
